lead_signal.headline: No new FIMI operations identified this week — see coverage notes.
lead_signal.confidence_basis: Exhaustive search across all Tier 1–3 named sources returned no new FIMI-specific disclosures, CIB reports, government attributions, or institutional publications dated May 9–15, 2026. Most recent actionable Tier 1 material is Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11, 2026) and Google TAG Q4 2025 Bulletin (January 29, 2026). No new TAG bulletin for Q1 2026 was found. No new MSTIC/MTAC influence-operations post was found for this week. No new EU EEAS EUvsDisinfo, Stanford IO, DFRLab, ASPI, Bellingcat, or EU DisinfoLab publications dated within the window were retrieved.
lead_signal.note: Quiet-week determination is based on absence of new primary-source disclosures within the May 9–15, 2026 window. The Meta H1 2026 report (March 2026) and Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin (January 2026) are the most recent Tier 1 anchors but fall outside the weekly window. Their findings are summarised in the weekly_brief_narrative for analytical continuity but are NOT treated as new-this-week signals. Confidence N/A is correct for a null lead signal per schema rules.

campaigns.operation_name: Iran IUVM / Endless Mayfly — Updated Attribution (Carry-Forward Reference)
campaigns.summary: Meta's H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11, 2026) updated attribution for the persistent Iranian influence operation 'Endless Mayfly' to Iran's International Union of Virtual Media (IUVM). A separate Iranian operation began targeting English-speaking audiences in summer 2025 and was disrupted by Meta in Q4 2025 before it could build an authentic audience. This is a carry-forward reference — no new disruption or disclosure was published in the May 9–15 window.
campaigns.new_this_week: No new developments this week. Carry-forward from Meta H1 2026 report (March 11, 2026) for analytical continuity.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: Persistent multi-platform CIB operations targeting post-Soviet periphery (Moldova, Armenia), Western Europe, and Ukraine narratives. Increasing use of unwitting freelance social media managers in Africa as operational cutouts. Portal Kombat domain network remains active across Google surfaces.
actor_tracker.headline: Russia remains the most documented FIMI actor across Tier 1 sources; no new disruption disclosed this week.
actor_tracker.summary: Meta and Google TAG Tier 1 disclosures from Q4 2025–Q1 2026 confirm Russia as the highest-volume FIMI actor across platforms. No new disruption or attribution was published in the May 9–15 window, but the operational posture assessed as unchanged.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: High-volume spam-and-pivot model (Spamouflage/Taizi Flood): mass account acquisition, spammy lifestyle content as cover, with a small subset pivoting to political narratives targeting US-China relations, Tibet, and Taiwan. Increasing sophistication noted in Meta H1 2026 report.
actor_tracker.headline: China assessed as one of three leading global FIMI sources; no new platform disclosure this week.
actor_tracker.summary: China-linked operations continue at high volume per most recent Tier 1 disclosures. No new CIB report or government attribution was published in the May 9–15 window.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: Iran continues cyber-enabled influence operations via IUVM and Cotton Sandstorm. Endless Mayfly attribution updated to IUVM in Meta H1 2026. New English-language operation targeting Western audiences disrupted in Q4 2025 before audience-building phase. IRGC EU terrorist designation (2023) has not visibly degraded operational tempo.
actor_tracker.headline: Iran's IUVM attribution confirmed in Meta H1 2026; no new disruption this week.
actor_tracker.summary: Iran assessed as active FIMI actor with confirmed IUVM attribution for Endless Mayfly. No new disclosure in the May 9–15 window. Structural OSINT gap on IRGC-linked operations outside Meta/Google visibility.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: Structural OSINT gap: Gulf-state FIMI operations are underrepresented in Tier 1–3 disclosures due to commercial relationships between Gulf states and Western platform infrastructure, and limited civil-society monitoring capacity in the region. No new disclosure found this week.
actor_tracker.headline: No new Gulf-state FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists.
actor_tracker.summary: Gulf-state FIMI activity is assessed as ongoing but underdocumented. Absence of disclosure does not equal absence of activity. Lower confidence is structurally correct, not an analytical failure.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: US-origin influence operations are structurally underreported in Tier 1–3 sources due to domestic legal constraints on attribution, platform reluctance to designate US-origin CIB, and the GEC's reduced public output posture in 2025–2026. No new disclosure found this week.
actor_tracker.headline: No new US-origin FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists.
actor_tracker.summary: US-origin FIMI activity cannot be confirmed or denied from available Tier 1–3 sources this week. Identical evidentiary standards applied; lower confidence is structurally correct.

actor_tracker.doctrine_note: Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin documented termination of 2 YouTube channels and 1 Ads account linked to Israel, sharing content in English critical of Canada. This is the most recent Tier 1 disclosure. No new disclosure found in the May 9–15 window.
actor_tracker.headline: Israel-linked YouTube channels terminated by Google TAG in Q4 2025; no new disclosure this week.
actor_tracker.summary: The most recent Tier 1 disclosure for Israel-linked FIMI is Google TAG Q4 2025 (January 29, 2026), documenting a small operation targeting Canada. No new material found this week. Identical evidentiary standards applied.

platform_responses.summary: Meta published its H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (first semiannual report under new DSA-aligned cadence), covering CIB operations from Russia, Iran, and China, updated IUVM attribution for Endless Mayfly, and disruption of a new Iranian English-language operation in Q4 2025. Report also covered scam center takedowns (10.9 million accounts in 2025) and AI-enabled abuse trends. This is the most recent Meta Tier 1 disclosure; no new Meta CIB action was published in the May 9–15 window.

Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin (last updated January 29, 2026) documented terminations of YouTube channels linked to Russia (targeting Moldova, Armenia, Germany/EU), Israel (targeting Canada), Pakistan, and Indonesia, plus domain blocks for Russia-linked Portal Kombat network. No new Google TAG bulletin for Q1 2026 was found in the May 9–15 window.

X/Twitter has no CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism. No transparency report, takedown notice, or influence-operations disclosure was published by X/Twitter in the May 9–15 window. Absence of disclosure is recorded separately from absence of activity per schema rules. Stanford Internet Observatory (2024) documented that X was slow to act on Meta-published threat indicators for Doppelganger-linked accounts.

No new MSTIC or MTAC influence-operations-specific publication was found for the May 9–15 window. Most recent MSTIC/MTAC FIMI-relevant material predates the window. Microsoft threat intelligence blog activity in this period focused on North Korean cyber actors (Sapphire Sleet) and phishing infrastructure, not FIMI.

cognitive_warfare.headline: AI-enabled FIMI tradecraft: increasing sophistication documented across Russia, Iran, China operations
cognitive_warfare.detail: Meta's H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 2026) noted that increasing sophistication and tradecraft was a cross-cutting theme in disruptions of covert influence operations from Russia, Iran, and China. The report also documented adversarial use of AI to violate Community Standards and Meta's use of AI to defend against adversarial abuse, including LLM-based detection of celebrity impersonations. Microsoft MTAC and MSTIC have separately documented AI operationalisation by nation-state actors to scale malicious activity. No new AI-FIMI-specific disclosure was published in the May 9–15 window.
cognitive_warfare.significance: The convergence of AI capability with FIMI tradecraft represents a structural shift in the cognitive warfare threat landscape. The Meta H1 2026 finding that AI-enabled operations are now a baseline feature — not an exception — across the three leading FIMI actors (RU/CN/IR) has direct implications for detection thresholds, attribution confidence, and platform enforcement capacity. The absence of a new weekly disclosure does not reduce the analytical significance of this trend.

cognitive_warfare.headline: EU regulatory rollback risk: digital omnibus package could enable more targeted disinformation campaigns
cognitive_warfare.detail: EU DisinfoLab's January 2026 newsletter flagged that the European Commission's digital omnibus package (presented November 2025) includes measures that could roll back data protection rules, potentially enabling more 'targeted' (tracking-based) advertising and more tailored disinformation campaigns. The Cyprus EU presidency (to June 2026) is expected to advance these proposals. No new development on this track was found in the May 9–15 window.
cognitive_warfare.significance: Regulatory rollback on data protection directly affects the structural conditions under which FIMI operations are conducted and detected. Weakening of tracking restrictions could expand the targeting surface available to state and commercial FIMI actors operating through advertising infrastructure.

attribution_log.headline: Meta updates attribution of Endless Mayfly influence operation to Iran's International Union of Virtual Media (IUVM)
attribution_log.summary: Meta's H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (published March 11, 2026) provided updated attribution for the persistent Iranian influence operation known as Endless Mayfly, linking it to Iran's International Union of Virtual Media (IUVM). This is a Tier 1 platform-level attribution based on Meta's internal technical investigation. MF2 flagged: Meta is the sole named attributing source in this search cycle; independent corroboration from Tier 2–3 sources was not retrieved in this session.

attribution_log.actor_or_campaign: IL — Israel-linked YouTube operation targeting Canada
attribution_log.headline: Google TAG terminates 2 YouTube channels and 1 Ads account linked to Israel, sharing English-language content critical of Canada
attribution_log.summary: Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin (last updated January 29, 2026) documented termination of 2 YouTube channels and 1 Ads account as part of an investigation into coordinated influence operations linked to Israel. The campaign shared content in English critical of Canada. Attribution is assessed (not confirmed) as Google TAG's standard bulletin language is 'linked to' rather than formally attributed to a state actor. MF2 flagged: Google TAG is the sole attributing source; no independent corroboration found.

attribution_log.actor_or_campaign: RU — Multi-platform CIB, Africa freelancer network
attribution_log.headline: Meta documents Russian use of unwitting freelance social media managers in Africa to advance CIB policy violations
attribution_log.summary: Meta's H1 2026 report disclosed that Russian CIB operations in Africa have evolved to use unwitting freelance social media managers as operational cutouts, advancing violations of Meta's CIB policy. This represents a documented evolution in Russian operational tradecraft — outsourcing content production to reduce attribution risk. Attribution confidence High based on Meta's Tier 1 technical investigation.

weekly_brief_narrative:
Week ending 15 May 2026 — Absence-of-Signal Assessment

This week's collection cycle returned no new FIMI-specific disclosures from any Tier 1, Tier 2, or Tier 3 named source within the May 9–15, 2026 window. The determination is based on exhaustive search across Meta Transparency Center, Google TAG blog, Microsoft MSTIC/MTAC, EU DisinfoLab, Stanford Internet Observatory, DFRLab, ASPI, and Bellingcat. No new CIB reports, government attribution statements, platform takedown notices, or institutional research publications dated within the window were retrieved.

The most recent Tier 1 anchor remains Meta's H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11, 2026), which confirmed Russia, Iran, and China as the three leading sources of foreign influence globally, with increasing sophistication and tradecraft across all three actors. Key findings from that report — updated IUVM attribution for Iran's Endless Mayfly operation, Russian use of unwitting African freelancers as CIB cutouts, and a new Iranian English-language operation disrupted in Q4 2025 before audience-building — remain the most current confirmed intelligence available. Google TAG's Q4 2025 bulletin (January 29, 2026) is the most recent platform-level takedown disclosure, documenting Russia-linked operations targeting Moldova, Armenia, and Western Europe, plus a small Israel-linked operation targeting Canada.

Platform transparency cadence note: Meta has shifted to semiannual reporting under DSA alignment, meaning the next scheduled Adversarial Threat Report is not expected until approximately September 2026. Google TAG bulletins are quarterly; no Q1 2026 bulletin was found in this search cycle, suggesting it may not yet have been published or was not indexed. X/Twitter continues to operate without a CIB-equivalent disclosure mechanism — absence of disclosure is recorded separately from absence of activity, consistent with schema rules. Microsoft MSTIC/MTAC activity this week focused on North Korean cyber actors and phishing infrastructure, not FIMI.

Structural coverage gaps this week: Gulf-state and US-origin FIMI operations remain structurally underrepresented in available Tier 1–3 sources. This is an analytical condition, not a finding of inactivity. The EU regulatory track (digital omnibus package, DSA enforcement cadence) warrants continued monitoring: EU DisinfoLab's January 2026 assessment flagged that proposed data protection rollbacks could structurally expand the targeting surface available to FIMI actors operating through advertising infrastructure.

Personnel watch returned no significant changes at any named organisation this week.

Analytical posture: The absence of new disclosures in a given week is not analytically equivalent to a reduction in FIMI activity. Platform reporting cadences (quarterly, semiannual) create structural gaps between operational activity and public disclosure. The three leading FIMI actors (RU/CN/IR) are assessed as maintaining their operational posture based on the most recent available evidence.

open_findings.headline: Meta shifts to semiannual adversarial threat reporting cadence under EU DSA alignment, creating a structural 6-month gap between CIB disclosure cycles beginning in 2026.
open_findings.why_it_matters: The shift from quarterly to semiannual Meta CIB reporting — explicitly linked to DSA Very Large Online Platform transparency requirements — means that active FIMI operations may run for up to six months before appearing in Meta's public disclosure cycle, reducing the signal density available to open-source analysts and creating a predictable operational window for adversarial actors who monitor platform disclosure cadences.
open_findings.source_name: Meta Transparency Center — H1 2026 Integrity Reports

open_findings.headline: Russia documented using unwitting freelance social media managers in Africa as operational cutouts for CIB, representing a documented evolution in attribution-evasion tradecraft.
open_findings.why_it_matters: The use of unwitting third-party contractors as cutouts complicates attribution chains, reduces the forensic footprint of state-directed operations, and creates plausible deniability at the operational layer — a significant tradecraft evolution that challenges both platform detection and legal attribution standards.
open_findings.source_name: Meta Transparency Center — H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report

open_findings.headline: EU digital omnibus package (November 2025) proposes data protection rollbacks that EU DisinfoLab assesses could enable more targeted disinformation campaigns via tracking-based advertising.
open_findings.why_it_matters: Regulatory changes to data protection infrastructure directly affect the structural conditions under which FIMI operations are conducted, detected, and attributed; a weakening of tracking restrictions expands the targeting surface available to both state and commercial FIMI actors operating through advertising ecosystems.

open_findings.headline: Google TAG Q4 2025 bulletin documents Israel-linked YouTube operation targeting Canada — a rare Tier 1 disclosure of a Western-aligned state's influence operation.
open_findings.why_it_matters: Israel-linked operations are structurally underrepresented in Tier 1 disclosures; this TAG bulletin entry is analytically significant as a rare instance of a Western-aligned state actor appearing in a platform-level CIB disclosure, and it tests the schema's requirement for identical evidentiary standards across all six monitored actors.
